---
title: "Probability Coding Project"
subtitle: "Naive Bayes Text Classifier from Scratch"
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    papersize: letter
    geometry:
      - margin=0.85in
    fontsize: 11pt
    colorlinks: true
    number-sections: false
    header-includes: |
      \usepackage{booktabs}
      \usepackage{amssymb}
      \usepackage{amsmath}
      \usepackage{enumitem}
      \setlist{nosep, leftmargin=1.5em}
      \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.35}
---

\begin{tabular}{@{}ll@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Time estimate} & \~{}1.5 hours \\
\textbf{Language} & Python (no sklearn --- just dicts and math) \\
\textbf{Output} & Working classifier + printed predictions \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}

\vspace{1em}

Build a text classifier using Bayes' theorem. No libraries --- just dictionaries, string splitting, and logarithms.

# The Math

Given a document with words $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n$, we want $P(\text{class} \mid w_1, \ldots, w_n)$.

Bayes' theorem:
$$P(c \mid w_1, \ldots, w_n) = \frac{P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid c)}{P(w_1, \ldots, w_n)}$$

The ``naive'' assumption: words are independent given the class. This is wrong but works surprisingly well.

Since the denominator is the same for all classes, we only need:
$$\text{score}(c) = \log P(c) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(w_i \mid c)$$

We work in **log-space** because multiplying many small probabilities causes underflow ($10^{-6} \times 10^{-6} \times \cdots = 0$ in floating point).

# Part 1 --- Build the Classifier (\~45min)

## 1a. `fit(texts, labels)`

- Count how many documents belong to each class $\to$ compute $P(c)$
- For each class, count word frequencies across all documents in that class
- Apply **Laplace smoothing**: add 1 to every word count (so unseen words get a small nonzero probability instead of zero)
$$P(w \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(w, c) + 1}{\text{total words in } c + |\text{vocabulary}|}$$

## 1b. `predict(text)`

- For each class, compute $\log P(c) + \sum \log P(w_i \mid c)$
- Return the class with the highest score

## 1c. `predict_proba(text)`

- Compute the log-scores for all classes
- Convert to probabilities using softmax:
$$P(c_j) = \frac{e^{s_j}}{\sum_k e^{s_k}}$$
- For numerical stability, subtract $\max(s)$ before exponentiating

# Part 2 --- Train and Test (\~30min)

## 2a. Create a dataset

Write 20 short movie reviews by hand (10 positive, 10 negative). Examples:

- \textit{``This film was absolutely brilliant and moving''} $\to$ positive
- \textit{``Terrible acting and a boring plot''} $\to$ negative

## 2b. Train and evaluate

- Train on 16 reviews, test on 4
- For each test review, print the prediction, the true label, and the confidence (probability from `predict\_proba`)
- Print the accuracy

## 2c. Inspect the model

- Print the top 5 most ``positive'' words and top 5 most ``negative'' words (highest $\log P(w \mid \text{pos}) - \log P(w \mid \text{neg})$ and vice versa)
- Try a review with a word not in the training set. Verify that Laplace smoothing handles it gracefully (small probability, not zero/crash)

# Part 3 --- Reflect (\~15min)

Answer in comments:

1. Why do we use log probabilities instead of raw probabilities?
2. What would happen without Laplace smoothing if a test document contains a word never seen in training?
3. Modern LLMs use the same $\log P(w \mid \text{context})$ pattern but with neural networks instead of word counts. What's the analogy?

# Deliverables

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Working \texttt{NaiveBayes} class with \texttt{fit}, \texttt{predict}, \texttt{predict\_proba}
  \item Printed predictions with confidence scores on the 4 test reviews
  \item Top 5 positive/negative words printed
\end{enumerate}
